coredata.dc:title: Composite molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for visual detection of ciprofloxacin in seawater
coredata.dc:creator: Wang, Na
Majid, Abdul
Wang, Kunpeng
Tan, Liju
Li, Huiru
Wang, Jiangtao
coredata.dc:description: As a commonly used antibiotic, the residues of ciprofloxacin (CIP) enter the environment owing to its abuse and it is of great significance for environmental protection and human health to develop a fast and selective sensor for the determination of trace CIP. In this work, composite imprinted ratiometric fluorescent nanoparticles of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs that can efficiently and rapidly detect ciprofloxacin (CIP) in seawater samples were synthesized by sol-gel method. The blue fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at 436 nm was the reference, and the orange yellow fluorescence of CdTe QDs at 547 nm was sensitive to CIP. The sensor showed satisfying linear relationship till to 20 μmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9963) with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μmol L−1 and recoveries of 96.2–103.1% in seawater. It was successfully applied to the detection of CIP in seawater after pretreatment by solid phase microextraction. The sensor exhibited a “green-blue” color change window visible to the naked eye under 365 nm UV light. This method provides a fluorescence imprinting strategy for the visualization and in situ detection of CIP in environmental water.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Ratiometric fluorescence
Sensor
Molecular imprinting
Ciprofloxacin
Seawater

Fluorescence response and sensitivity of the sensor
Selectivity and stability of the sensors

This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41876078) and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Marine Ecological Environment Science and Engineering, Ministry of Natural Resources (No. MESE-2019-06).

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Schematic illustration for preparation of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors and detection of CIP.
Effect of pH (A) and dosage of sensor (B) on the fluorescence intensity of sensor. All CIP concentrations were 10 μmol L−1.

Fluorescence spectra of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs (A), CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs (C) and CdTe CQDs@MIPs (D), Fluorescence intensity ratio of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor in relation to CIP with the concentration range of 0.5–20 μmol L−1 (B).
Selectivity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor for CIP and its structural analogs and ions (10 μmol L−1). All experiments were performed in pH = 7 buffer.
Recovery tests of spiked CIP in seawater.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: ND=Not detected. Each result is the average of three repetitions.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Performance of the new probe comparison with other reported methods for detecting CIP.

head.ce:title: Composite molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensor based on carbon dots and CdTe quantum dots for visual detection of ciprofloxacin in seawater

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Marine Chemistry Theory and Technology, Ministry of Education, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China

ce:correspondence.ce:text: Corresponding author. College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering, Ocean University of China, Qingdao, 266100, China.

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: College of Chemistry and Chemical Engineering

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: As a commonly used antibiotic, the residues of ciprofloxacin (CIP) enter the environment owing to its abuse and it is of great significance for environmental protection and human health to develop a fast and selective sensor for the determination of trace CIP. In this work, composite imprinted ratiometric fluorescent nanoparticles of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs that can efficiently and rapidly detect ciprofloxacin (CIP) in seawater samples were synthesized by sol-gel method. The blue fluorescence of carbon quantum dots (CQDs) at 436 nm was the reference, and the orange yellow fluorescence of CdTe QDs at 547 nm was sensitive to CIP. The sensor showed satisfying linear relationship till to 20 μmol L−1 (R2 = 0.9963) with limit of detection (LOD) of 0.09 μmol L−1 and recoveries of 96.2–103.1% in seawater. It was successfully applied to the detection of CIP in seawater after pretreatment by solid phase microextraction. The sensor exhibited a “green-blue” color change window visible to the naked eye under 365 nm UV light. This method provides a fluorescence imprinting strategy for the visualization and in situ detection of CIP in environmental water.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Ciprofloxacin (CIP) is a third-generation quinolone synthetic antimicrobial drug that is widely used for its wide-spectrum antimicrobial activity [1]. Its bactericidal effect is satisfactory, not easy to develop drug resistance, and it has superior performance in the treatment of various bacterial infections such as organism urinary system infection, respiratory system infection and systemic soft tissue infection [2–4]. The wide application of CIP raises the potential for drug residue hazard. The research suggested that up to 70% of the unmetabolized excreta of CIP can promote the formation of resistance in microbial populations when it is released into the environment [5]. People pay more and more attention to antibiotic pollution in water environment, and it has gradually become a global problem. CIP has been detected in domestic water, underground water and seawater in recent years. The presence of long-term low concentrations of CIP may have an impact on microbial community in water bodies and affect higher organisms through the transmission of the food chain, which in turn affects humans [6,7]. It is more common in developing countries where a significant portion of the direct discharge of spontaneous as well as non-treated drugs occurs [8]. CIP is widely used in aquaculture in China, and humans may acquire undesirable reactions and chemical resistance after eating these aquatic products [9]. Thus, detection of CIP in water samples is important for human life safety.

Although ciprofloxacin itself has fluorescent properties, it is difficult to detect CIP in water samples directly by fluorescence under the influence of other components [10]. In recent years, high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) combined with UV or mass spectrum (MS) is a common method to detect CIP in water samples [11–13]. Although these large instruments and techniques can achieve satisfactory accuracy and resolution in detection, disadvantages such as high cost and operational complexity severely limit their widespread use, especially in resource-limited field monitoring situations. Molecular imprinting techniques allow the preparation of highly selective, sensitive and low-cost molecular polymers that provide unique molecular recognition sites to identify targets [14–16]. Recently, fluorescence detection technology has attracted the attention of researchers, and the method has the advantages of high sensitivity, wide applicability and moderate cost [17,18]. The combination of fluorescence detection with molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) results in improved sensitivity and specificity of the detection. Lian et al. [19] reported the development of a highly selective processing method based on molecularly imprinted solid-phase extraction. Liu et al. [20] developed a sensitive CdTe quantum dots (QDs) fluorescence sensor for detecting CIP in seawater by wrapping molecularly imprinted polymers (MIPs) on the surface of CdTe quantum dots (QDs). However, single emission molecular imprinted fluorescence sensors are easily affected by the operating environment, and ratio fluorescence method can effectively alleviate these interferences and improve the sensitivity of fluorescence detection by two fluorescence signals for self-correction. Carbon dots (CQDs) are widely used in fluorescence detection as a new fluorescent material with wide source, friendly environment and excellent optical performance, which has many advantages such as stable optical performance, low toxicity and simple preparation process [21]. CdTe QDs are still widely used in optoelectronic applications because of their extremely high quantum yields. In addition, CdTe QDs are potentially valuable in sensors, life sciences, and displays as very efficient fluorescent materials that can provide aesthetics for a variety of applications while having stable luminescence properties [22]. The construction of the sensor based on CQDs and CdTe QDs as the fluorescence signal can avoid the interference of background factors and optimize the stability, accuracy and photocatalytic degradation of the detection.

In this work, a composite molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescent sensors was constructed for detection of CIP in seawater. First, CQDs with small particle diameters and uniform sizes were synthesized by a one-pot method [23]. Then, CQDs@SiO2 was obtained by coating a silica layer using the sol-gel method and orange yellow-emission CdTe QDs was also prepared in advance. Molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensors (CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs) were prepared by combining CQDs@SiO2 and CdTe QDs by sol-gel method, with CQDs providing blue fluorescence for the reference signal and CdTe QDs as the response signal to provide orange yellow fluorescence. As a result, the sensor provided a self-correcting function. This sensor has been successfully used for visual detection of CIP in seawater after preconcentration by solid phase microextraction, demonstrating the potential application of molecular imprinting ratio fluorescence technology in detecting CIP in seawater.

Citric acid monohydrate (C6H8O7·H2O), o-phenylenediamine (C6H8N2), norfloxacin (NOR), erythromycin (ERY) were provided by Aladdin Industrial Corporation (Shanghai, China). sodium borohydride (NaBH4), tellurium powder (Te), 3-aminopropyltriethoxy silane (APTES), cadmium chloride (CdCl2·2.5H2O), tetraethyl orthosilicate (TEOS), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ammonium acetate buffer solution, malachite green (MG) and melamine (MEL) were purchased by Sinopharm Group Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd (Shanghai, China). 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA) and sulfadiazine (SDZ) were provided from Maclean Biochemical Technology Co. Ciprofloxacin (CIP) and chloramphenicol (CAP) were purchased from Beijing Solexpro Technology Co (Shanghai, China). Polydimethylsiloxane (PDMS)-coated SPME polar sorptive extraction stirring bar purchased from Beijing Guojiahengye Scientific Instruments Co. All chemicals are analytical grade.

The transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEM-F2000, Japan) were used to characterized the morphology of CQDs, CQDs@SiO2, CdTe QDs and CdTe/CD@SiO2@MIPs. Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectroscopy was recorded on FT-IR spectroscopy (Nicolet iS50, Thermo Fisher Scientific Corporation). Spectrophotometer (Shimadzu UV-2500, Japan) was used to record UV–vis spectra. Freeze drying was performed on a freeze dryer (FD-1A-5D, China).
The process of synthesizing CQDs is provided in the supporting information.

CQDs@SiO2 was synthesized by sol-gel method. Briefly, 5 mmol of APTES was added to 20 mL of CQDs solution, which was stirred with heat for 30 min. APTES-coated CQDs were then obtained. After that, 10.0 mL of the above mentioned substances, 2.0 mL of APTES, 1.0 mL of TEOS, and 2.5 mL of NH3·H2O were mixed to the ethanol solution, which was stirred for 12 h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation and dried under vacuum.
CdTe QDs was synthesized according to previous literature [24] and it was provided in the supporting information.

For the preparation of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs, 10 mL of CdTe QDs and 0.5 mL of APTES were mixed with 50 mL of Milli-Q water under ultrasonic conditions and then stirred for 0.5 h 10 mg of CQDs@SiO2, 16 mg of CIP and 2 mL of TEOS were dispersed into the above solution under vigorous stirring for 30 min. 0.1 mL of NH3·H2O was added to initiate the reaction and the solution was stirred at room temperature for 24 h. Finally, the products were collected by centrifugation, washed with methanol and Milli-Q water alternately, and dried under vacuum at 4 °C. In contrast, the non-imprinted polymer (CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs) was prepared by the same process but without the addition of CIP, and the imprinted polymer (CdTe QDs@MIPs) was prepared by the same process but without the addition of CQDs@SiO2.

Fluorescence spectra were obtained using an F-4600 fluorescence spectrophotometer for optical detection analysis. Slit widths of 10 nm were used for excitation and emission. Excitation wavelength was set to 365 nm, and emission was recorded in the range of 390–700 nm. CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs, CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs and CdTe QDs@MIPs (all 600 mg L−1) were mixed separately with solutions of 0.5–20 μmol L−1 CIP solution or seawater samples. The pH was adjusted to 7 using a borax-borate buffer solution, and the fluorescence was detected after 4 min of reaction.

The feasibility of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs for CIP detection was validated by seawater samples. The seawater samples were taken from the offshore waters of Qingdao, and the seawater was filtered with 0.45 μm acetate membrane for impurity removal. 6 mg of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs and 10 mL of treated seawater were mixed in a centrifuge tube, and the fluorescence intensity at two wavelengths was measured. Then 6 mg of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs and 10 mL of seawater samples containing 5, 10 and 15 μmol L−1 CIP, respectively, the pH was adjusted to 7 with borax-boric acid buffer solution, and the fluorescence intensity was obtained after ultrasonic. The pre-concentration procedure for SPME according to previous studies with necessary modification and shown in the supporting information [25].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Preparation and characterization of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The procedure of the molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor constructed was illustrated in Fig. 1 . Blue fluorescent CQDs were first synthesized by the one-pot method, then CQDs coated with silica (CQDs@SiO2) were synthesized by the sol-gel method. This SiO2 coating stabilized the blue fluorescence of the CQDs and bound them to another imprinted layer. 3-mercaptopropionic acid (MPA)-modified CdTe QDs were synthesized by the aqueous phase synthesis method under the environment of oxygen removal. Then molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs) with specific imprinted cavities were synthesized by sol-gel method using CIP as template molecule, APTES as functional monomer, NH3·H2O as initiator and TEOS as cross-linker [26,27]. After synthesizing CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@ MIPs, the template molecule CIP, which was bound to the sensor through hydrogen bonding, was eluted with methanol. The imprinted cavities complementary to CIP in size, shape and chemical groups were generated on the surface of the polymer layer. These imprinted cavities had selectivity and can recognize CIP again by hydrogen bonding. The orange yellow fluorescence of the sensor quenched after recognizing CIP, while the blue fluorescence remained stable. Under the irradiation of a 365 nm ultraviolet lamp, the sensor indicated a color evolution from green to blue.

By an analysis of the UV–vis spectrum of the eluate, the number of washings that can completely remove the template was explored. As shown in Fig. S1A, the UV intensity of CIP gradually decreased as the number of elution increased. After five times of elution, the UV intensity of CIP in the eluent was almost zero, indicating that the template molecule CIP has been completely removed. In Fig. S1B, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs (c) was significantly lower than that of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs (a) before the template CIP was eluted. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs significantly increased to 96% of the original level after elution. Above results showed that CIP could be completely eluted after six elutions.

The morphologies of nano-CQDs, CdTe QDs, CQDs@SiO2 and CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs were investigated by TEM. As illustrated in Fig. S2A, the diameters of CQDs were approximately 5 nm, showing obvious uniform dispersion and spherical particles. The CdTe QDs (Fig. S2B) also showed uniformly round particles with size close to that of CQDs. CQDs@SiO2 (Fig. S2C) showed a significant increase in particle size after coating with SiO2. CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs formed larger polymers with particle size larger than 100 nm, indicating the formation of an imprinted layer (Fig. S2D).

The various functional groups and chemical bonds present in the polymer were characterized by FT-IR spectra to further verify the synthesis of the CQDs, CQDs@SiO2 and CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs (Fig. S3). The characteristic peaks at 1066 and 1038.5 cm−1 belonged to asymmetric stretching vibration of Si–O–Si. The bands at 786, 794, 440.7 and 448 cm−1 were assigned to the Si–O vibrations, and these results indicated the effective formation of SiO2 shell layers [28–31]. The bands at 3269, 3372 and 2932 cm−1 could be ascribed to C–H and N–H stretching vibrations, indicating that the –NH2 group of APTES was attached to the SiO2 layer surface of CQDs@SiO2 and CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs [32].
All the above characterization results confirmed the successful preparation of the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor.

To achieve highly sensitive detection of CIP, it is necessary to optimize the key parameters that influence the interaction of CIP with the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor, including pH, amount, response time, and fluorescence stability.

The pH of the solution is a significant factor affecting the morphology and adsorption capacity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor in aqueous solution. To investigate the effect of pH on the fluorescence quenching efficiency of the sensor, the pH was controlled in the range of 2–12 as CIP concentration at 10 μmol L−1. The maximum fluorescence quenching of sensor was observed at pH 7 (Fig. 2 A). At low pH, the protonation of the amino group on the MIPs layer reduces the recognition ability of the binding site to CIP in the form of cation. On the other hand, at high pH (>7), both APTES on the MIPs layer and CIP exist in the form of anions, resulting in mutually exclusive electrostatic interactions that make it difficult for CIP to approach the binding site and reduce fluorescence quenching [33]. At the same time, alkaline conditions can cause ionization of the surface silicon shell layer, disrupt the integrity of the shell layer, and cause the imprinted cavities as defects that suppress the ability of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor to adsorb CIP [34].

The amount of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor will affect the fluorescence quenching efficiency. Therefore, the amount of sensor needs to be optimized to achieve the optimal sensitivity of the sensor. The fluorescence responses of different amounts of sensor (100–1000 mg L−1) were measured at pH = 7.0. In Fig. 2B, the sensitivity of the sensor was highest at the amount of sensor of 600 mg L−1. A smaller amount of sensor cannot provide an enough fluorescence intensity, and also limits the number of binding sites leading to inaccurate measurement results. As the amount of sensor is too larger, it causes a waste of experimental materials and may also cause self-absorption of fluorescence between sensor, which affects the accuracy of fluorescence detection. Therefore, the final dosage of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was chosen to be 600 mg L−1.

The recognition of CIP by the sensor and its entry into the imprinted cavities is a mass transfer process that requires a certain amount of time. After the addition of the CIP, the fluorescence intensity ratio of the two emission wavelengths decreased rapidly and the fluorescence quenching reached the maximum value at 4 min (Fig. S4). In the following 6 min, the fluorescence intensity remained basically unchanged. Thus, a complete response can be achieved within 4 min, indicating a rapid mass transfer between the fluorescence sensor and the CIP and which was mainly attributed to the large number of recognition sites and imprinted cavities on the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor surface. Therefore, 4 min was chosen as the response time of the sensor.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence response and sensitivity of the sensor
ce:section.ce:para: The recognition capability of the sensors is the most important step in this experiment. The sensitivity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs, CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs and CdTe QDs@MIPs was investigated in the range of 0.5–20 μmol L−1 CIP under the above optimized conditions. The fluorescence spectra of the three sensors were shown in Fig. 3 . The fluorescence intensity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs at 547 nm decreased continuously and the fluorescence intensity at 437 nm remained almost unchanged with addition of CIP, resulting in a decrease in orange yellow fluorescence and unchanged in blue fluorescence with a “green-blue” color change window visible to the naked eye under 365 nm UV lamp irradiation. On the contrary, the fluorescence intensity of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs were slightly decreased at 547 nm with the same quantity addition of CIP. The fluorescence intensity of CdTe QDs@MIPs decreased, but the quenching efficiency was lower than that of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs. It was worth noting that although the blue fluorescence intensity of CQDs at 437 nm was unchanged in the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs and CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@NIPs, but appeared a weak blue shift, which was probably due to the fluorescence effect of the CIP. In conclusion, the above results indicated that the molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor improved the sensitivity to the target analyte CIP and significantly improved the quenching efficiency. This was due to the presence of a high number of imprinted cavities on the surface of the polymer, which allowed for specific binding to the target substance. Compared to single fluorescence sensors, ratiometric fluorescence sensors allow quantitative detection of target analytes based on the establishment of an internal standard with higher sensitivity while eliminating interference from other factors.

Fluorescence quenching phenomenon can be performed by the Stern-Volmer equation:
(F 547/F 436)0/(F 547/F 436) = 1+K
SV [C]
Here, (F 547/F 436)0 is the fluorescence intensity ratio without agent and (F 547/F 436) with agent, K
SV is the Stern-Volmer bursting constant and [C] is the concentration of quenching agent. As shown in Fig. 3B, the linear equation was y = 0.0580x+1.0055 (R2 = 0.9963) when CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was exposed to the concentration range of 0.5–20 μmol L−1 CIP. The CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs fluorescence intensity ratio showed a linear relationship as the CIP concentration increased. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from 3δ/b to be 0.09 μmol L−1 (δ is the standard deviation of the blank sample and b is the slope of the calibration curve). Thus, CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor offer advantages in terms of sensitivity and visualization [35].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity and stability of the sensors
ce:section.ce:para: The selectivity of the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was further evaluated by fluorescence detection [36]. As shown in Fig. 4 , the fluorescence quenching effect of the sensor with the addition of CIP was significantly stronger than that of structural analogs or ions. This demonstrated the strong selectivity of the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor for CIP. This phenomenon can be explained by the fact that the surface of the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor has many specific recognition sites for CIP, which allows CIP to bind specifically to the sensor and it leads to fluorescence quenching. Although NOR can also bind to APTES by hydrogen bonding, the intensity of the fluorescence quenching response was much lower than CIP. Therefore, it caused a much smaller fluorescence quenching than CIP. These phenomena further demonstrate the high selectivity and specificity of the sensor for specific detection requirements.

In order to judge the fluorescence stability of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor, the sensor was stored at 0–4 °C and protected from light. The fluorescence intensity ratios were measured after every three days under the same conditions. The experimental results were shown in Fig. S5. The fluorescence intensity ratios of sensor did not differ significantly in 13 days, showing satisfactory photostability, which may be caused by the good protection of quantum dots by the silicon shell of MIPs. The results indicated that the material had good physical stability and chemical inertness.

By eluting the imprinting material with a mixture of 100 mL of acetic acid and methanol (1:9, v/v), the reusability of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor was tested. After elution, the sensor was subjected to fluorescence detection in the solution containing CIP (10 μmol L−1) again, and the above procedure lasted for 6 cycles. As shown in Fig. S6, the (F547/F436)0/(F547/F436) values in the first four cycles remained basically unchanged, all of which remained above 95% of the initial fluorescence ratio. After four cycles, the (F547/F436)0/(F547/F436) values of the sensor began to significantly decrease, and the fluorescence quenching degree gradually decreased. This may be due to multiple elutions disrupting the integrity of the CIP binding point and imprinting cavities, which reduced the selectivity of the target analyte. Therefore, the prepared CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor in this experiment can be cycled up to 4 times.

The reason for fluorescence quenching generated by CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor is mainly due to electron transfer or energy transfer between the sensor and CIP. As shown in Fig. S7A, the fluorescence emission spectrum of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor did not overlap with the UV–visible absorption spectrum peak of CIP, which proved that there was no possibility that the fluorescence of the sensor was absorbed by CIP, and excluded energy transfer of the mechanism. It was found that the UV spectra of CIPs overlaps with the UV–vis spectra of CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs to a large extent (Fig. S7B), which confirmed the existence of electron transfer between the sensor and CIP. It was speculated that there was hydrogen-bond interaction between NH2 group of CIP and functional monomer on the sensor surface, which may lead to electron transfer from conduction band of sensor to lowest unoccupied molecular orbital of CIP, resulting in fluorescence quenching of the CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs sensor.

Regrettably, the detection limit (LOD) of the sensor is 0.09 μmol L−1, which is higher than the CIP concentration of 0.033 nmol L−1 in collected seawater sample determined by HPLC-MS combined solid phase microextraction, so it cannot be directly used to detect CIP in actual seawater [37]. CIP in the seawater sample was enriched using stir bar solid phase microextraction which can concentrate targets over 3000 times in seawater [38,39]. The sensor showed satisfying test results with concentration detected of 0.04 nmol L−1 after pre-concentration of 3000 times. It demonstrated that the sensor can successfully detect CIP in seawater after combining with stir bar solid phase microextraction.

A composite molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence sensor (CdTe/CQDs@SiO2@MIPs) were constructed successfully based on CQDs and CdTe QDs and applied to the visualization and detection of CIP in complex seawater under the optimal conditions explored experimentally. The sensor identified CIP by the quenching of orange yellow fluorescence and the blue fluorescence stabilized, so the sensor showed a “green-blue” color evolution window visible to the naked eye as the CIP concentration increased. The sensor performed well in terms of sensitivity, selectivity and visualization compared to conventional sensors. The successful preparation of this sensor provides a simple, versatile and visualized alternative method for detecting CIP in seawater. Therefore, molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence technology has promising applications for rapid in situ visual detection of various target analytes and the development of molecularly imprinted ratiometric fluorescence technology for the application would be promoted.

Na Wang: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Data curation. Abdul Majid: Visualization, Investigation, Data curation. Kunpeng Wang: Resources, Project administration, Investigation. Liju Tan: Software, Methodology. Huiru Li: Visualization, Investigation. Jiangtao Wang: Writing – review & editing, Supervision, Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was supported by the National Natural Science Foundation of China (No. 41876078) and the Open Fund of Key Laboratory of Marine Ecological Environment Science and Engineering, Ministry of Natural Resources (No. MESE-2019-06).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.mssp.2023.107800.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Automatised on-line Spe-chiral Lc-Ms/Ms method for the enantiomeric determination of main fluoroquinolones and their metabolites in environmental water samples

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Mejias C., Luis Santos, J., Martin J., Aparicio I., Alonso E. Automatised on-Line Spe-Chiral Lc-Ms/Ms Method for the Enantiomeric Determination of Main Fluoroquinolones and Their Metabolites in Environmental Water Samples[J]. Microchemical Journal, 2023, 185.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Adsorption of ciprofloxacin from water: a comprehensive review

ce:bib-reference.ce:source-text: Igwegbe C. A., Oba S. N., Aniagor C. O., Adeniyi A. G., Ighalo J. O. Adsorption of Ciprofloxacin from Water: A Comprehensive Review[J]. Journal of Industrial and Engineering Chemistry, 2021, 93: 57-77.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Modulating the oxidative active species by regulating the valence of palladium cocatalyst in photocatalytic degradation of ciprofloxacin
